It'll be a little interrupt in this presentation. It's a hierarchy of human motivation. And Maslow knew that some of the things that we as human beings are pretty much very compelled to do, irregardless of culture or time or anything else, we tend to all have to aspire to the ultimate being self-actualization. And so it's interesting that he's talking about something that people, they would normally be considered a preference or a trade or kind of something else but he actually talks about it as a need. And it really does make sense once you get through it. And if you think about it that way, the interesting part is you'll find when you go through Maslow, you start looking at everything and everyone, every movie, every person you encounter, that person that's freaking out in the movie theater about something or the person on the freeway or doing something else, you can see Maslow at work and you go, well, he doesn't really have to be acting that way. And he's like, actually Maslow makes it that that person is going to behave that way pretty reliably. But the interesting about the human organism that's what Maslow says a lot here is that we have these different preferences. It's not all rigid. Only the bottom of the pyramid is very rigid on our behavior. Something else is kind of a cocktail of whatever it is that you have going on in your life and what your preferences are. So some things higher up in the pyramid get weighted differently. So he calls it very dynamic motivations and that's a very important thing to know. But there's still these general motivations. So I don't see my notes showing up, but hopefully they'll show up. Okay. So most of us were brought up to believe that reason and emotion are polar opposites with reason as a stalwart white hat and emotion as the sulky black hat. And let's not even discuss which gender was said to wear which hat. Reason it was thought sees the world as it is. While the irrational scam emotion tries to undermine it. Uh-huh. Turns out as neuroscience writer Jonah Lairer says, if it weren't for emotions, reasons wouldn't exist at all. Take that play. Oh, this is illustrated by a sad story that even Sadler, it's a real life protagonist doesn't see a sad at all because he can't literally. Elliot, a patient of Antonia DiMazio had lost. See that change? A small section of his prefrontal cordus is during surgery for a benign brain tumor. Before his illness, Elliot held a high level corporate job and had a happy thriving family. By the time he saw DiMazio, Elliot was in the process of losing everything. He still tested in the 97th percentile in IQ, had a high functioning memory and had no trouble enumerating each and every possible solution to a problem. Trouble was he couldn't make a decision from what color pen to use to whether it was more important to do the work his boss expected or spend the day alphabetizing all the folders in his office. Why? Because as DiMazio discovered the damage to his brain left him unable to experience emotion. As a result, he was utterly detached and approached life as if everything in it was neutral. But wait, shouldn't that be a good thing? Now that emotion couldn't butt in and cloud Elliot's judgment, he'd be free to make rational decisions, right? I think you know where this is going. Without emotion, each option carried the exact same weight. Everything really was six of one half a dozen of the other. So it turns out as Cognitive Scientist Steven Pinker notes, emotions are mechanisms that set the brain's highest level goals. Along with apparently every other goal down to what to have for breakfast. Without emotions, Elliot had no way to gauge what was important and what wasn't. What mattered and what didn't. It is exactly the same when it comes to story. If the reader can't feel what matters and doesn't and doesn't, then nothing matters, including finishing the story. The question for writers then is where do these feelings come from? The answer is very simple. The protagonist. So this is why all these feelings and emotions, we're going to get into it more. I found a good, okay. So this is, we're moving on to Maslow and this is his hierarchy. Okay. The more we're at the bottom here, the more we have a predictable behavior. The more we pretty much don't have a choice of what we prioritize. And as we go up, it becomes more and more optional, so to speak. So, so, and these are kind of loose categories. You see air, water, food, shelter, sleep, clothing. They're all kind of in a similar race, but they all trump or what would he call as a prepotent or prepotent ownership over the person's behavior before they can move on to this one. So basically there's no 100 percentile, but when you go from one to the other, you have to have a, at least a majority of the needs in each of the categories taken care of before the average person will be able to move up to this level. And they have to have an average of this taken care before they move up to the next and so on and so on and so on. The other thing I want to add, I know, it's like, I want it for brevity, I want to cut them all out, but I was like, God, those are so damn good, I can't leave any of them out. And the other thing I want to say here now that we're reminding is one of the things that you do is if you go through a painful story, like with Gary Halbert, he would go the amazing diet secret of a desperate housewife from Ohio. And the thing is, is that tells you that you're going to hear a painful story, but it also tells you you're going to have a happy ending. And that's a big difference for a person that knows it's going to get better, because if you're drawing the pain that someone else has exactly like yours, they'll keep reading, because they're drawn into it. But if they feel there's no hope at the end, there's no reason to read that. That's just making their life miserable. So keep in mind when you do something like that, that if you're going to take a parallel path with the reader, that they know that there's a light at the end of the tunnel and it's not a train. Okay. And so just remember the Wired for Story readings, all connected. These all these things chase each other. Okay. Now, one, in every insecure person with whom the writer has worked, he has always found a continual, never dying longing for security. Sometimes this has been conscious, sometimes unconscious and unrecognized by the subject. Furthermore, there were found wide variations in the subject's individual definitions of security that they long for. Some defined it in terms merely of safety. Some in terms of dependence upon a stronger person. Some in terms of having power and money. And a few defined it in what is a more correct way, i.e., a longing to deserve and win back the love and affection of other people. So miscellaneous are these various longings that if we were to be really conservative, we should say only that there was a response of dissatisfaction and a longing for a more satisfactory state. This is more satisfactory state being defined individually. However, it is fair to say with at least three levels of satisfactoriness. We're going to get to those. One, safety. Two, belongingness and three, receiving love and affection. All very powerful. Very, very powerful motivators. Okay. Almost continual action towards regaining this individually divine security was found in all cases. So a lot of times we know this, that if you take something away, they'll fight more to get it back if they had it to begin with. Okay. So however, this is long for state was defined, however this long for state was defined, the individual almost always did something to try to achieve it. Okay. Discouragement. Number three, discouragement reactions may be a response to failure of these attempts or to further threats or to further threats to security to introspective moods. To invidious comparisons with others. Or anything else that drives home the realization of rejection, being hated, loss of love, etc. These discouragement reactions will be found frequently if one looks deeply enough for them. Sometimes it will become very obvious indeed. This was abbreviated so that's why... This is where you would address previous failures of your prospects attempts to achieve security, to read and still hold. Describe their failure in detail so that they know you have something that to offer that isn't the same thing they've been offered before. Okay. Moving on. Okay. Revenge reactions for love already lost or for a threatened loss. Here we may speak of the general aggressiveness and hostility of the insecure person of his bitterness, his antagonism, his intendance, his tendency to hate everyone. Defense reactions prevent further loss of love. The realization that a certain kind of behavior leads to rejection may lead to inhibition of this behavior or even reaction formations, e.g. the person realizing consciously or unconsciously that stinginess will be disapproved by other people may not stop being stingy but may become excessively generous or extravagant. We may include here also the various flight or avoidance reactions which usually are largely defensive. Okay. Trying to get all this as fast as possible. Okay. Ameliordiative reactions to make the bad situation more bearable. Just hold on. Check our chat. Make sure everything's cool. Okay. To make that more bearable. Here we may have the sour grace mechanism in which there may be conscious attempts to save oneself from hurt by changing attitudes toward that which was desired. Here an individual may say after all there are many advantages in not having too many friends or not being married. Thus he may attempt to make the best of a bad situation. Again, this is objection raising that you guys might want to bring up depending on what you have to sell. Okay. Attack reactions upon the situations which bring about the insecurity in an attempt to change or improve them. This tech may be literally example physical attack upon a person or it may be general social radicalism to change the factors and side that bring about insecurity such as unemployment, exploitation, etc. The attack reaction may be good. That is a realistic one but it may also be a blame reaction, a protective one in which the individual attempts to save his tattered self-esteem by blaming others completely when he's himself is also the blame. Blame reactions may be paralyzing since they tend to make it impossible to win back the real security by making it more difficult for the person to analyze the situation so it may be improved. Here's the thing is that a person that is so entrenched in this is probably going to quickly embrace somebody who says the same thing. You know that misery loves company. If you find a guy that blames the whole world for his situation on this factor, this person, this set of people, blah blah blah, you start speaking the same way. You've got a person that's like ready to bond with you. You have a friend, a brother in arms so to speak. You know what I mean? Okay. The undermining self-esteem. Self-esteem and insecurity although they are separable variables tend to correlate with each other. Any attack on security tends to also be an attack on self-esteem. Very much so. Very true. That goes without saying I don't need to make any comment. Okay. The insipidition will tend to always hide from himself as long as possible the conscious realization of rejection. This is denial of grace, right? Of loss of love and respect. This is another kind of defensive reaction. In the person who has not yet given into his feelings of insecurity, who has not given up struggle, we should find this always to be so. That is he will feel more insecure unconsciously than he is consciously. This is important because you need to talk to... Is it the little voice in his head or are you talking to his voice up front? A lot of times when you're talking to someone's core dominant resident emotions, you can't go straight to the back room. That'll make him very uncomfortable. So you have to be very aware of whether you're talking to his front politically correct side or his socialized values or whether you're talking to what his real core dominant emotions are and whether you can speak directly to it. Consciously, he may overlook completely a slight, a snub, a rejection, but this is a very apt to come out in his dream that night or in hypnosis. Another way of saying this is that a person who is still fighting against his insecurity will show a certain optimism. The very opposite will be the case with the person who has given up. He will show the reaction of pessimism. He will be far more conscious of the snubs than of the compliments. He will, so to speak, believe the snub and not believe the compliment. This reaction will be found at times even in the person who has not given up when he's blue, when he's tired, when he's drunk, when he's asleep, etc. And that's the thing is that you find that certain things that people experience color their whole world, colors their whole world outlook. So I got a little good. I didn't. For some reason I have a note here. I don't know why I wrote it, but it's to remember Dan Kennedy's problem agitates solve. You guys can think of why I wrote that. It would be fine. Okay, sorry guys. At least you're all laughing. Okay. All right. Other processes that perpetuate the adjustment or the lifestyle. Every adjustment tends to become high in internal consistency to be less anthropomorphic in every insecure person. There tends to be a change in his a perceptive mass in his world philosophy. He's saying what I was saying. Okay. He looks out upon the world in an insecure way. Thus he tends to be blind to all the facts that run counter to his conviction that he is insecure. He tends to remember only insecure memories. He tends to disparage traits of security within himself. He tends to always go toward the hundred percent end of the scale. We can see this in politics and religion, all kinds of things. From mistrusting 75% of all people, he tends to slowly come to mistrust 80 to 90% and so on until he tends to mistrust all people. I've met a guy like this. Just like this. I could have dialed him in perfectly. Okay. If something happens that conflicts with his interpretation of the world, he tends to twist this happening to reinterpret it so that it fits into his world scheme. I love this stuff. Okay. Thus if he thinks that all people are essentially selfish and then observe someone behaving unselfishly, he will tend to manufacture or project selfish motives upon this unselfish person. What does this mean for the understanding of the insecure individual? As he becomes more secure in one department of life, in one area, he may expect the automatic tendency towards insecurity in all departments of life and his thinking, his perceptions, his remembering, his forgetting, his emotions, his philosophy. This is also true in the type of areas of security defined by Horowitz. The person who becomes insecure in his relation to his employers tends to also inexorably become insecure in all social areas in the sexual life, in his relations to his family, etc. Okay. The effects of all these reactions on the other people serve further to perpetuate and reinforce the insecurity. Horny has described this well in the concept of the vicious circle. Thus, if the insecure person is mistrustful, his mistrust changes his behavior willy-nilly in such a way so that other people become mistrustful of him. This in turn intensifies his mistrust, which in turn intensifies their mistrust. Okay. And so on. The person who for whatever reason hates other people will make them hate him, which will make him hate them even more, etc. Contrary wise, the person who loves other people is loved more by them for easily discernible reasons. We've seen this with John Rowley was on the show. He's a total, he's a total optimist. This confirms and strengthens his love for other people, which confirms their love for him. Okay. Okay. Limiting the base of security or safety. Frequently enough, we observe that what seems at first to be a paradoxal form of security, example, in Dahlia's description of lower class Negroes in southern towns and overprotected children and Goldstein's brain injured cases, this is written in the 40s, just so you guys know, that's why this is talking about it. These are people who maintain an apparent security simply by narrowing the effective world which they live. This can be done by completely giving one's autonomy up to someone else, the slave or the overprotected child. By lowering the level of aspiration very markedly or living only in that area of life, which is absolutely familiar, safe or predictable, is this way a kind of safety or pseudo safety can be attained, which we shall call security on a narrow base. I'm going to get into an example. So long as there is someone else to solve all problems for the individual or so long as he stays within the restrict, his restricted world, or so long as he asks for nothing and hopes for nothing, just so long he will retain his apparent security. This security obviously depends entirely on a continuation of the necessary conditions. With these necessary conditions or disturbed consistency, then the individual is thrown into anxiety, hostility, neurotic reactions, helplessness and the like. Think of a baby. Real security is security on a broad base. That is, it must not crash in the face of the ordinary exidencies of life. Okay, so I've notes here. It says, one example of this is, and I don't know if it's still true, is that in the Japanese culture, when you signed up for a corporation, you were in that corporation for life. It was your sense of security, but it was also kind of, you were kind of cornered into what you were going to have as a life, as a person. I think even the book John Collins wrote, and Donnie wrote, the Incorporated Man, was about how their lives are. You get in that corporation and that's your life. And we'll get into it later, is that in that culture, as far as I know, you're not supposed to try to stand above the crowd and make a spectacle or self or be all about yourself and stuff like that. But we'll get into it that culturally, no matter what, with those little base needs, no matter what culture is trying to tell you how you should behave, you're trying to follow a Maslow to stay within those core motivations of Maslow. Those are underlying everybody's motivations, no matter what is on top, influencing people. Let's see. Okay. And then I wrote, I feel like I'll get up to this because I really think we're helping people. Does that all make sense? Does everybody get that? You know, I think it would also, I get it, but I think it also would help to start giving a few examples of how this comes to play, specifically how you can do it in marketing, not only have been done, but what you can do. So I want to give just a couple if you don't mind. That's fine. Like I said, if we go along, we can roll into another. One of the first cases to really using Maslow's hierarchy of needs, if you can do it, is to actually go ahead and understand and admit the things that are going on with yourself. One of the key fundamental, really key tools that any marketer has is the ability to step into the shoes of the prospect and understand what they're thinking and what they're saying. And so when you, you know, and I'm going to give you an example of this. And I just want to give an example of what Kevin was talking about with their needs and their hidden needs and their acceptance in groups and stuff like that. And I'm going to use a watch for it. There are a lot of people who buy high end watches and the real reason, the real underlying reason that nobody wants to admit is because they want a brat. You know, they want to show off their watch. It's a big piece of jewelry. So what they will give the front PC, that you know, good ad will give the front, give them a lot of front loaded excuses that they can tell their friends that are based on keeping them happy in their groups and not standing, you know, why you're not going to look like a fool when you've got this. And you do that with the stories and you do it with the stories of what you were doing with it, but you give them their, you know, this is made of the 40, you know, 400 man hours are put into the production of every watch and so forth. And that's all the PC stuff and the reasoning that they can go around and share and be comfortable that they're not going to be pulled out of their comfort zone by buying a watch that everybody's going to say, Hey, you know, why'd you spend so much waste, so much money on the wrist clock as they call. But when you want to hit those undervalued, those underlining things, which is, which you don't want to admit, which is because you'll be able to brag and show it off and rub your wealth into everybody's face. They don't want to say that. But what you can do is you can say, you tell those subliminally in the story. You say, you know, and then after, actually, after I got it, I started noticing, I was, I would get seated at a better spot in the restaurant. And all of a sudden it was a little easier for people to take me seriously. These are all the acceptance in those group things that you want it, that they want to be elevated to that you and the reason core emotions behind it that you don't want to admit that, and they don't want to admit. Our first proposition states that the person is old. Our first proposition states that the individual is an integrated, organized whole. The theoretical statement is usually accepted piously enough by psychologists, who then often proceed calmly to ignore it in their actual experiments. That is an experimental reality, as well as a theoretical, that is an experimental reality as well as a theoretical one must be realized before sound experimentation and sound motivation theory are possible. In motivation theory, this proposition means many specific things. For instance, it means the whole individual is motivated rather than just part of him. In good theory, there is no such entity as a need of the stomach or mouth or a general need. There is only a need of the individual. It is John Smith who wants food, not John Smith's stomach. Furthermore, satisfaction comes from to the whole individual and not just to part of him. Food satisfies John Smith hunger and not his stomach's hunger. And that's the important thing to know. Dealing with John Smith's hunger as a function merely of his gastrointestinal tract has made experimenters neglect the fact that when an individual's hungry, he changes not only his gastrointestinal functions, but in many, perhaps even most other functions of which he is capable. His perceptions change. He will perceive food more readily than he will at other times. His memories change. He's more apt to remember a good meal at this time than at other times. His emotions change. He's more tense and nervous than he is at other times. The content of his thinking changes. He is more apt to think of getting food than solving an algebraic problem. And this list can be extended to almost every other faculty, capacity or function, both physiological and psychic. In other words, when John Smith is hungry, he's hungry all over. He is different as an individual from what he is at other times. He is all about the food. I'm just looking at my notes here. Just taking a second. Translate. If we examine carefully the average desires that we have in daily life, we find that they have at least one important characteristic, i.e. That they are usually means to an end rather than ends in themselves. Keep this in mind. We want money so that we may have an automobile. This is features and benefits that will be coming up in the future with us. In turn, we want an automobile because the neighbors have one. We want to watch, right? And we wish not to feel inferior to them so that we can retain our own self-respect so that we can be loved and respected by others. Usually when a conscious desire is analyzed, we find that we can go behind it, so to speak, to other more fundamental aims of the individual. In other words, we have a situation which parallels very much the role of symptoms in psychopathology. Symptoms are important, not so much in themselves, but for what they ultimately mean, that is, for what their ultimate goals or effects are, for what they are trying to do, or for what their functions may be. The study of symptoms in themselves is quite unimportant. But the study of the dynamic meaning of symptoms is important because it is fruitful, for instance, making possible psychotherapy. The particular desires which pass through our consciousness dozens of times a day are not in themselves so important as what they stand for, what they lead to, what they ultimately mean upon deeper analysis. I'm reading a bedtime story. Okay. There is now anthropological evidence to indicate that the fundamental or ultimate desires of all human beings do not differ nearly as much as do their conscious, everyday desires. Our undercurrents are all the same. The main reason for this is that two different cultures may provide two completely different ways of satisfying a particular desire, let us say for self-esteem. In one society, one obtains self-esteem by being a good hunter, and another society by being a great medicine man or a bold warrior or a very unemotional person and so on. It may be that if we think of ultimates, the one individual's desire to be a good hunter has the same dynamics and the same fundamental aim as the desire of the other individual to be a good medicine man. We may then assert that it would be more useful for psychologists to combine these two seemingly disparate conscious desires into the same category rather than to put them into different categories purely on behavioral grounds. Apparently, ends in themselves are far more universal than the roads taken to achieve those ends, for these roads are determined locally in the specific culture. So when people always come to Gary and say, but my customers are different, and he'd be like, no, they're not. My customers are sophisticated. My customers are educated. My customers are... This statement of propotency is a statement of average findings in a large sampling of our population. This propotency means primarily that where all the wants are unsatisfied, the one that will tend to dominate consciousness and behavior will be the search for... Conscious and behavior will be the search for gratification. He has only the main conscious goals of getting food, water and most often makes the naive assumption that if he's satisfied in these wants, he will then be happy and contented. But this is not so. When the most dominant want is satisfied, a new one emerges into consciousness. Usually the next higher in the hierarchy of propotency. If this second want is satisfied, then the third one appears from nowhere. Ordinarily, the satisfaction of these wants are not altogether mutually exclusive, but are so only as a matter of degree. The average member of our society is probably partially satisfied and partially dissatisfied in all of his wants. The hierarchy principle is then observable in terms of increasing percentages of dissatisfaction as we go up the hierarchy. So you're more likely to have all your needs taken care of at the bottom level. You're less likely to have your needs taken care of on the average of population to the top level. All right. It's been pointed out that these physiological drives or needs are to be considered unusual rather than typical because they are isolable and because they are localizable somatically. That is to say they're relatively independent of each other of other motivations and of the organism as a whole. And secondly, and in many cases, it is possible to demonstrate a localized underlying, underlying somatic base for the drive. This is true less generally than has been thought. Exceptions are fatigue, sleep, uh, maternal responses, but it is still true in the classic instances of hunger, sex and thirst, which I, I nod my head to that one big time. Okay. If all the needs are unsatisfied, the organism is then dominated by the physiological needs. All other needs may become simply non-existent or be pushed into the background. It is then fair to characterize the whole organism by saying simply that it is hungry. For consciousness is almost completely preempted by hunger. All capacities are put into the service of hunger, satisfaction, and the organization of these capacities almost entirely determined by the one purpose of satisfying hunger. We're going to get into, as a level, the drive is kind of the same for, for more abstract things. The receptors and effectors, uh, the intelligence, memory habits, all may now be defined as simply as hunger, gratifying tools, capacities that are not useful for this purpose, like dormant or pushed in the background. The urge to write poetry, the desire to acquire an automobile, the interest in American history, the desire for a new pair of shoes are in the extreme case forgotten or become secondary of importance. For the man who is extremely endangered, no other interest exists but food. He dreams food. He remembers food. He thinks about food. He moats only about food. He perceives only food and he wants only food. The more subtle determinants that ordinarily fuse with the physiological drives in organizing, even feeding, drinking or sexual behavior, now may be so completely overwhelmed as to allow us to speak at this time, but only at this time, a pure hunger drive and behavior with the one unqualified aim of relief. Another particular characteristic of human organism when it's dominated by a certain need is that the whole philosophy of the future tends to also change. For our chronically and extremely hungry man, Utopia can be defined very simply as a place where there is plenty of food. Think of a oasis in the desert. He tends to think that if only he is guaranteed food for the rest of his life, he will be perfectly happy and will never want for anything more. Life itself tends to be defined in terms of eating. Anything else will be defined as unimportant. Freedom, love, community, feeling, respect, philosophy, may all be weighed aside as fripperies which are useless since they fail to fill the stomach. Such a man may fairly be said to live by bread alone. All right. I'm not boring you guys too much. This is going to be important. Okay. One main implication of this phrasing is that the gratification becomes as important a concept as deprivation in motivation theory for it releases the organism from the domination of a relatively more physiological need, permitting thereby the emergence of other more social goals. Physiological needs along with the partial goals when chronically gratified cease to exist as active determinants of or organizers of behavior. Then now exist only in a potential fashion in the sense that, well, okay, they now, I'm once distracting me, they now exist only in a potential fashion in the sense they may emerge again to dominate the organism if they are thwarted, but a want that is satisfied is no longer a want. They may serve as them, almost exclusive organizers of behavior, recruiting all the capacities of the organism in their service. Okay. One thing that must be stressed at this point is that love is not synonymous with sex. Sex may be studied as a purely physiological need. Ordinarily, sexual behavior is multi-determined. The esteem needs. Okay, we're getting up there. Let's probably get into our Rolex. Watch. Okay. All people in our society with a few pastological exceptions have a need or desire for a stable, firmly based, usually high evaluation of themselves for self-respect or self-esteem and for the esteem of others. By firmly based self-esteem, we mean that which is soundly based upon real capacity, achievement and respect from others. These needs be classified into two subsidiary sets. These are, first, the desire for strength or achievement, for adequacy, for competence in the face of the world and for independence and freedom. It's all gold. It's all gold stuff. Okay. That you can sell the world. Okay. Secondly, we have what we may call the desire for reputation or prestige, defining it as respect or esteem for other people. From other people. Sorry. Recognition, attention, importance. Importance or appreciation. Perhaps the desire for prestige and respect from others is subsidiary to the desire for self-esteem or confidence in oneself. Observation of children seems to indicate that this is so, but clinical data gives no clear support for such a conclusion. These needs have been relatively stressed by Alfred Adler and his followers and have been relatively neglected by Freud and the psychoanalysts. More and more today, however, there is a peering widespread appreciation of the central importance. On page, oh, we're on 97 and 120. Secondly, we have what we call the desire for reputation or, didn't I just read that one? Yes. I must have a double slide. I get that. I had so much trouble with my PowerPoint. Okay. Satisfaction of the self-esteem needs leads to feelings of self-confidence, worth, strength, capacity and adequacy of being useful and necessary in the world. But thwarting of these needs produces feelings of inferiority, weakness and helplessness. These feelings in turn give rise to either basic discouragement or else compensatory or neurotic trends. An appreciation of the necessity of basic self-confidence and an understanding of how helpless people are without it can be easily gained from a study of so severe traumatic neurosis. Okay. Still strong stuff. Okay. Need for self-actualization. We're getting, all right. Even if all the needs are satisfied, we may still often, if not always, expect that a new discontent and restlessness will soon develop unless the individual is doing what he is fitted for. And we're back to our thing. I'm a musician, a musician must make music. An artist must paint. A poet must write. If he is to be ultimately happy, what a man can be, he must be. This need we may call self-actualization. This term, first coined by Kurt Goldstein, is being used in this paper in a much more specific and limited fashion. It refers to the desire for self-fulfillment. Namely, to the tendency for him to become actualized in what he is potentially. This tendency might be phrased as the desire to become more and more what one is to become everything that one is capable of being. Reminds me of be all you can be. Okay. I have any notes on that one? No. Okay. The specific form that these needs will take, will of course vary greatly from person to person. This will get much more diversity. In one individual may take the form of desire to be an ideal mother. In another, it may be expressed athletically. And still another, it may be expressed in painting pictures or in inventions. It is not necessarily a creative urge, although people who have any capacities for creation, it will take this form. Okay. Wow, we're really almost to the end. You guys can interrupt me anytime. We're at 103 of 120 slides. The preconditions for the basic need satisfactions. There are certain conditions which are immediate prerequisites for the basic need satisfaction. Danger to these is reacted to almost a zip or a direct danger to the basic needs themselves. I know this to be true. Such conditions as freedom to speak, freedom to do what one wishes. So long as no harm is done to others, freedom to express oneself, freedom to investigate and seek for information, freedom to defend oneself, justice, fairness, honesty, orderliness in the group are examples of such preconditions for basic needs satisfaction. I like that he's talking about these are being needs. So wording in these freedoms will be reacted to with a threat or emergency response. These conditions are not ends in themselves, but they are almost so since they are so closely related to the basic needs, which are apparently the only ends in themselves. These conditions are defended because without them, the basic satisfactions are quite impossible or at least very severely endangered. So got a lot of passion all the way up the scale. It's almost like the degree of fixity on the hierarchical, a basic hierarchy, a basic needs. I'll have some slide notes I want to talk about a little bit after too. We have spoken so far as if this hierarchy were a fixed order, but actually is not nearly as rigid as we may have implied. It is true that most of the people with whom we have worked have seen to have these basic needs in about the order that has been indicated. However, there have been a number of exceptions. There are some people in whom, for instance, self-esteem seems to be more important than love. This most common reversal in the hierarchy is usually due to the development of the notion that the person who is most likely to be loved is a strong or powerful person, one who inspires respect or fear and who is self-confident or aggressive. Therefore, such people who lack love and seek it may try hard to put on a front of aggressive, confident behavior, but essentially they seek high self-esteem and its behavior expressions more as a means to an end than for its own sake. This is the whole thing is about what's the means to the end. They seek self-assertion for the sake of love rather than for self-esteem. So when we're selling products, we're selling a product as a means to one of Maslow's ends. I hope people can understand that if you think deep enough and long enough, you're going to find that whatever your marketing ends up somewhere in the pyramid. I think that's a good point, Kevin. And that last slide. I'll go back. One of the criticisms about Maslow's hierarchy is that the brain doesn't think hierarchically. It's kind of more horizontal. It thinks of all of those things at the same time. So that kind of ties in with this slide that you've got just now. And the other thing I was going to ask is, what do you think about that 98% of everything they know about the brain, they've discovered in the last five years? Do you think that all of this is still relevant? I think Maslow would be loved to be alive today. That book, the one book I recommended was called the Female Brain. And it's really, it tells how the female brain works, but the way it tells it works, it has to tell how the male brain works. So it talks about how both sides work. And it's all done through fMRI studies. Well, not just that. They also do blood check levels of oxytocins and hormones and stuff like that. And it's amazing. I mean, you get all these people that are upset about the idea that, oh, love is just a chemical response. And you read the book and you're like, damn straight it is. Culture specificity and generality of needs. This classification of basic needs makes some attempt to take account of the relative unity behind the superficial differences and specific desires from one culture to another. Certainly, in any particular culture, an individual's conscious motivational content will usually be extremely different from the conscious motivational content of an individual in another society. However, it is the common experience of anthropontologists that people, even in different societies, are much more alike than we would think from our first contact with them, and that as we know them better, we seem to find more and more of this commonness. We then recognize the most starling differences to be superficial rather than basic. Example differences in style, hair, hair dress, clothes, tastes, in food, etc. Our classification of basic needs is in part an attempt to account for this unity behind the apparent diversity from culture to culture. No claim is made that it is ultimate or universal for all cultures. The claim is made only that it is relatively... Yes. And Maslow, everybody knows Maslow started something. He did perfect it. You know, as Mark was talking about, he was just... It was just like, oh, you know, someone's getting this. Okay. Um, let's see. Let's see. Good. Oh, sorry. Okay. Okay, more ultimate, more universal, more basic than superficial conscious desires from culture to culture and make somewhat closer approach to common human characteristics. Basic needs are more common human than superficial desires or behaviors. Okay. I don't know. That's taken a little out of context, but I think you get the idea. Another important point to discuss fully in a subsequent publication is that there is a basic difference between expressive behavior and coping behavior. Functional striving, propulsive goal-seeking. An expressive behavior does not try to do anything. It is simply a reflection of the personality. A stupid man behaves stupidly. I don't necessarily believe in all this with the new FMRI study. Not because he wants to or tries to or is motivated to, but simply because he is what he is. The same is true when I speak in a base voice rather than a tenor soprano. A random movement of a healthy child to smile on the face of a happy man, even when he is alone. The springiness of a... Of the healthy man's walk and the erectness of his carriage are other examples of expressive non-functional behavior. Also the style in which a man carries out almost all his behavior, motivated as well as un-motivated, is often expressive. I believe there's more going on behind this than he said back then. Okay. Here is a good... My notes say this is a good part coming up. Okay. So it is such considerations as the that suggests the bold postulation that a man who is thwarted in any of his basic needs may fairly be envisioned simply as a sick man. Like I look right on my screen now. This is for a fair parallel to our designation as sick of the man who lacks vitamins or minerals. Who is to say that lack of love is less important than a lack of vitamins? Since we know the pathogenic effects of love starvation. Who is to... and this is all FMRI stuff. Who is to say that we are invoking value questions in an unscientific or illegitimate way? This guy's back in the 40s. Any more than the physician does who diagnoses and treats halagra or scurvy. If I were permitted this usage, I should then say simply that a healthy man is primarily motivated by his needs to develop and actualizes fullest potentialities and capacities. If a man has any other basic needs in any active chronic sense, then he is simply an unhealthy man. Okay, so... Okay, he is as surely sick as if he had suddenly developed a strong salt hunger or calcium hunger. If we're to use the word sick in this way, we should then also have to face squarely relations of man to his society. One clear implication of our definition would be that one, since a man is to be called sick who is basically thwarted. And two, since such basic thwarting is made possible ultimately by the human being. And three, since such basic thwarting is made possible ultimately by forces outside the individual. Then three, sickness in the individual must come ultimately from a sickness in the society. This is not socialist stuff. The good, healthy society would then be defined as one that permitted man's highest purposes to emerge by satisfying all's prepidant basic needs. I think it's great going forward with an idea that what you have to offer people as a theme is medicine for people. And if you look at what the masal is doing, people are never... people are always in need of the medicine. You just have to find out what medicines they've already got, what ailments they've already been taking care of, and what's next on their menu, on their list. Okay? So... See, I wrote my notes. This is huge because if what you offer helps fulfill this basic... what you offer helps fulfill this... I can be considered medicine. And I also note this is more of a mental attitude of the copywriter. The tone they can use, not literal words they write. So if you can come up with this passion, this belief, this conviction of what you're offering people is medicine, you'll be able to sell it with more conviction. Okay? There are at least five sets of goals which we call basic needs. These are briefly... briefly we've talked about these before. Physiological, safety, love, esteem, self-actualization. In addition, we are motivated by desire to achieve or maintain the various conditions upon which these basic satisfaction rests and by certain more intellectual desires. We're almost on the fourth slide, so... These basic goals are related to each other being arranged in a hierarchy of propotency. This means that the most propotent goal will monopolize consciousness and will tend of itself to organize the recruitment of the various capacities of the organism, the person. The less propotent needs are minimized, even forgotten or denied. But when the need is fairly well satisfied, the next propotent higher need emerges, in turn, to dominate the conscious life and to serve as the center of organization of behavior, since gratified needs are not active motivators. Thus, a man is perpetually... is a perpetually wanting animal. Ordinarily, the satisfaction of these wants is not altogether mutually exclusive, but only tends to be. The average member of our society is most often partially satisfied and partially unsatisfied in all he or his wants. The hierarchy principle is usually empirically observed in terms of increasing percentages of non-satisfaction as we go up the hierarchy. We talked about mostly satisfied on the bottom, mostly unsatisfied at the top. Any thwarting or possibility of thwarting of these basic human goals or danger to the defenses upon which to protect them or to the conditions upon which they rest is considered to be a psychological threat. With a few exceptions, all psychopathology may be partially traced to such threats. A basically thwarted man may actually be defined as a sick man. It does little good to say of a person that he feels that nobody loves them, if we do not at the same time recognize that automatically connected with this feeling are various responses and reactions which are practically always found when this feeling is found. I think we've covered this slide. Example, attempts to win back love, uncutting of the self-esteem, feelings of hatred and aggression towards the people, who are responsible, etc. This is then our connection between the feelings we have described and the motivational light. These feelings are also motives at one and the same time. Thwarting twice, for sure. Karen Halberst Cocktail and Must Read Books. Everything Gary Halbert, scientific advertiser, wired for story, the female brain and Maslow's hierarchy needs. But the way they value their car keys, because now they found them or their wallet or their glasses or their missing child or anything else, they could have been fine the whole time. Even if just relating to people, understanding people, dealing with people, dealing with family relatives, your job, your boss, everything fits in this thing. It explains why people do the stuff they do. Well, we made it through. Awesome. Kevin, I can say as a fellow copywriter, although not with nearly your experience, the shout out to people based on their varying levels and types of needs, sometimes you've got a product that can apply to lots of different markets and perhaps you're emailing to a list that contains all of them. And you might not be able to parse that out. We've had particular luck with being able to, it's literally, it's a bird shot thing. I wouldn't recommend doing it a lot of the time, but you can say if this is your issue. Then you need this. And if this is your issue, then you need this. And David Franklin, you guys remember him from the Welcome webinar. I worked with David quite a bit and he is masterful at that. If you need to make more money, you need to watch this video. If you need to quit your job, you need to watch this video. If you need to, you know, it is one after the other. And if you hit all of the potential needs in there, eventually you're hit the one that the reader is God, if you can keep them that long. And that is one of the coolest things. I know we have a lot of people here that are interested in copy or are writing copy. And it's amazing to me, you would think that the intellectual mind would kick in and go, well, he's just trying to hit everybody. It's just after everybody. But it really doesn't. They find the thing that applied to their level of need and blocked out everything else. It works really, really well. Yes, that's what bullets are all about. Bullets, I mean, which I know what you're talking about. You're talking about a headline that sucks them into the page. But if you get to the page, you know, there's been talk many times that there would be 30 bullets in a sales page, but only one of them needs to sell. Sometimes people only buy on one thing. There was one that my dad wrote as a bullet was like something like fake cocaine. So really, then some someone else can't tell or something. It was written for an entrepreneur magazine or something. And they had the phones ringing off the hook and everything was selling, all based on one bullet, one little bullet. And yes, people will come in and they will often buy off of one thing. If you can get to the Maslow thing, or you can describe, I would say probably two things, max. You can describe two things, max, that can be covered in a single sentence. That's probably it. I wouldn't try to go any farther than that. Yeah, but if you can, you have to find that common point and get to it. And you could also do that with maybe the leading and the leading subject headline or with the other piece. But yeah, that's a toughie. It's a toughie, it just depends on, I guess, how disparate the two markets you're trying to hit are. Because if I'm trying to sell this computer rig with four monitors to a gamer and a Wall Street guy, yeah, I'm going to have to either dilute it and make it more general. Or I don't do that. That's a good, that's a toughie. That's a toughie. I mean, I would just have to talk about how great it is at one thing that they both value. But then again, that may not be the number one hot button. It may be the number three hot button for all of them, for both sides, but it may not be the number one. You know, there may be two totally different number one hot buttons for both people. So yeah, it's definitely a tricky balance. Cool. Well, does anybody else on our panel have any questions or conversational notes with regard to Kevin's tremendously informational presentation here? Not for me. I think you did a good job of explaining it quite thoroughly, Kevin. Okay. Well, Maslow did most of the work. We did get one really interesting comment. Okay. That's much a question just to comment. It was if you're on a boat in the middle of the ocean, I've got the only bottle of water. You're going to pay me everything for that bottle of water. Absolutely. Absolutely. You know what? I have a different example, and that's a guy that's stuck inside the hull of a boat. He's got a bar of gold that he found, but there's a hole, and I've got an oxygen bottle I can trade him a gold for. He will trade me every bar of gold he's got for that one thing of oxygen. And that's another thing is that when you talk about that, that's another thing to talk about, which is you're not going to sell a bottle of water to a guy that is standing next to the Niagara Falls. You're going to sell it to the guy that's on the ocean and stuff like that. And the other thing is, Gary talked about starving crowds. It's like, how much are you starving? Maslow talks about why you're eating. Are you eating because you're hungry? Are you eating because you love the taste of food? There's two different, two different, totally different things. But the question becomes, what's their state? How starving are they in this one area? And so Gary, Howard, was very smart to say a starving crowd because he could say people, humans that like to eat and that wouldn't work. It was their state of thwart, as Maslow would call it, but not being satisfied or how level of pain. Alright, well guys, we have gone just about two hours on this one. Perfect. Wow.
